title: Failed Settlement Repair Ticketing
description: A reconciliation and back-office workflow that resolves breaks, validates accounting alignment, and closes audit-critical exceptions. Focus case: failed settlement repair ticketing.
assumptions: Each repair_ticket (T1) represents a single reconciliation break tied to one settlement_batch (T10) or a discrete reconciliation_scope (T3).
GL account (T5), counterparty_account (T4) and payment_instrument (T6) are distinct entities and must not be conflated when creating links or proposed corrections.
Supporting documents (T11) and external responses (T9) are treated as immutable evidence artifacts once attached to a ticket; approvals reference those artifacts.
Assignment availability (predicates on T2) is orthogonal to assignment relation; releasing an analyst returns availability to the pool.
Proposed corrections (T13) are staged and require explicit approval/escalation before ledger changes are recorded.
risks: Ambiguous parameter naming across actions may lead to misuse of resources if implementers reuse generic identifiers for different types; preserve unique tokens per type to avoid type confusion.
Incorrect linking of GL accounts or counterparty accounts may produce incorrect remediation entries and fail audit reconciliation.
Stale or missing supporting_document artifacts can block closure and generate repeated escalations.
Over-reliance on manual steps (assignment/release) can delay resolution and increase operational exposure for time-sensitive settlements.
If approval and escalation flows (R12/R15/R16 family) are not enforced, corrections may be applied without required governance, increasing compliance risk.
